item.title: Turla STOCKSTAY Targets Ukraine as Cellebrite Russia Use and Packagist Malware Blocking Emerge
item.content:encoded:
Google Details Turla's New STOCKSTAY Backdoor Used in Ukraine Espionage Attacks — Must Read

Google says Russian state-backed Turla is using a newly documented .NET backdoor called STOCKSTAY for espionage against Ukrainian government and military targets and entities tied to European foreign policy. The malware overlaps with Turla’s Kazuar toolkit and has been delivered through phishing, malicious RDP files, MSI installers, and WinRAR exploit chains.

  • Google Threat Intelligence Group attributed a previously undocumented .NET backdoor called STOCKSTAY to the Russian state-sponsored threat actor Turla.
  • STOCKSTAY has been deployed against government and military organizations in Ukraine and entities with an interest in Italian foreign policy.
  • The malware consists of multiple components, including STOCKSTAY.MARKETMAKER, STOCKSTAY.STOCKBROKER, STOCKSTAY.STOCKTRADER, and STOCKSTAY.STOCKMARKET, which communicate via Windows inter-process messages and a secure WebSocket connection.
  • STOCKSTAY.STOCKTRADER supports commands for file deletion and upload, directory enumeration, screen capture, process execution, Windows Registry operations, system information gathering, and ZIP extraction.
  • Google said STOCKSTAY shares significant code, functional, and architectural overlaps with Turla’s Kazuar backdoor, raising the possibility that both tools were developed and maintained in part by the same developer or team.

Russia Used Cellebrite on Jailed Activist's iPhone Months After Sales Cutoff — Should Read

Citizen Lab found that Russian authorities used Cellebrite UFED tools to extract data from jailed opposition activist Andrey Pivovarov’s seized iPhone in June 2021, months after Cellebrite said it had stopped sales to Russia. The case shows that sales cutoffs can be ineffective when older offline forensic hardware remains usable by government agencies.

  • Citizen Lab reported that Russian authorities used Cellebrite UFED forensic tools to access detained opposition activist Andrey Pivovarov’s iPhone in June 2021, three months after Cellebrite said it would stop selling tools and services to Russia and Belarus.
  • Pivovarov’s iPhone 12 and MacBook were confiscated when he was pulled off a flight at St. Petersburg airport on May 31, 2021, and he did not consent to a search or provide passwords.
  • MobileLockdown records on the iPhone showed a June 17, 2021 connection to a host ID matching a Cellebrite fingerprint, which Citizen Lab assessed as high-confidence evidence of UFED use.
  • A Russian forensic report given to Pivovarov named Cellebrite’s UFED Physical Analyzer and UFED 4PC and documented searches for Open Russia Civic Movement and named opposition figures.
  • Cellebrite told Citizen Lab and Access Now that any use of its legacy hardware in Russia after March 2021 was entirely unauthorized and said Russia remains on its restricted-customer list.

Packagist is now protected by Aikido Intel and other updates to the PHP registry — Should Read

Packagist and Composer are adding default supply-chain protections for PHP, including Aikido Intel-powered blocking of known malicious package versions in Composer 2.10 and mandatory stable version immutability to stop re-tagging attacks. PHP developers should update Composer to benefit from these protections.

  • Aikido’s Intel feed now powers malware blocking in Composer downloads and is enabled by default for Composer 2.10 and higher.
  • Composer uses flagged package metadata to remove malicious package versions from dependency resolution for commands such as composer update, composer require, and composer create-project, and composer install fails if a locked version is later flagged.
  • Packagist introduced stable version immutability so a published stable version can no longer be rewritten, preventing re-tagging attacks like the one described against Laravel-Lang.
  • Private Packagist can require a current Composer client to connect and can refuse to serve flagged version files to any client, including older Composer versions.
  • Packagist plans additional security improvements including minimum release age, organizational ownership for mandatory MFA, and staged releases.
